reasoning: As of October 2025, temperature tracking groups like Berkeley Earth, NOAA, and Copernicus estimate a near-zero probability that 2025 will be the hottest year on record. Although January 2025 was the warmest January on record, the subsequent months of 2025 have consistently ranked 2nd or 3rd warmest—falling behind the record-breaking months of 2024 (and occasionally 2023)—due to the transition away from the strong El Niño conditions of 2023–2024 into a moderate La Niña pattern. The Berkeley Earth temperature update in late October 2025 explicitly states there is a "next-to-nothing chance" for 2025 to surpass 2024 globally. Consequently, the data clearly projects 2025 will not be the hottest year according to NASA GISTEMP, leading to a confident minimum allowed probability forecast.
